coredata.dc:title: Dual sensitization effect and conductive structure of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst towards superior photodegradation activity for bisphenol A under visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoxu
Wang, Run
Lu, Ziyang
Wang, Weichao
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we prepared Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (the mesoporous TiO2 denoted as mTiO2) photocatalyst via a simple synthetic method, and it exhibits strong light response under visible light region because of the dual sensitization effect of Fe3O4 and carbon. In addition, the composite photocatalyst also has double conductivity, which is beneficial to the transfer and separation of charge carriers due to the synergetic effect of Fe3O4, carbon and mTiO2. As a consequence, the double conductive structure Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst displays prominent photocatalytic activity in degradation of BPA under visible light. Furthermore, the photocatalyst not only was recycled easily to reduce the secondary pollution of the environment but also had a superior stability. This work may provide a new inspiration toward constructing dual sensitization and double conductive structure with other semiconductor photocatalyst to effectively improve the photocatalytic activity under visible light for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon
Fe3O4
Mesoporous TiO2
Bisphenol A
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National key research and development program ( 2016YFB0901600 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21407059 , 21407064 and 21606114 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20140527 , BK20140532 and BK20150536 ), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. 1501102B ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M641638 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the magnetic core-shell structured Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst.
Possible photocatalytic mechanism of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample.
XRD patterns of different samples: Fe3O4@ mTiO2, Fe3O4@mTiO2/C, mTiO2 and Fe3O4.

TEM images of Fe3O4@mTiO2 (a, b), Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (c), Fe3O4@TiO2 (d) HRTEM image of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (d), FFT pattern of Fe3O4@mTiO2 (inset of e) and the corresponding EDS mapping of C (f), Fe (g), O (h) and Ti (i).
UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples: mTiO2, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C.
XPS spectra of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample: (a) Fe 2p, (b) Ti 2p, (c) C 1s and (d) O 1s.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution (inset) of the different photocatalysts: (a) mTiO2, (b) Fe3O4@mTiO2 and (c) Fe3O4@mTiO2/C.
Hysteresis loops recorded at 300 K of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (inset: separation of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C from solution under an external magnetic field).
The absorption spectra variations (a) of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample, dynamic curves of degradation BPA (b) plots of -ln(C/C0) versus time (c) and rate constant k (d)over different samples.
The five reaction cycles of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst for degradation of BPA under visible light (a) and the XRD patterns of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample before and after photocatalytic reaction (b).

Trapping experiments of the active species during the photocatalytic degradation of BPA in the presence of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (a), DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra with Fe3O4@mTiO2/C in the dark and under visible light irradiation for hydroxyl radical (b) and superoxide radical (c).
PL spectra (a) and fluorescence decay curves (b) of Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C samples.
Photocurrent response (a) and EIS spectra (b) of the different samples.
m/z of degrading BPA over Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst.
Proposed photocatalytic degradation pathway of BPA over Fe3O4@mTiO2/C under visible light.

head.ce:title: Dual sensitization effect and conductive structure of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst towards superior photodegradation activity for bisphenol A under visible light

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst is first constructed by a simple and eco-friendly strategy, which presents the excellent photocatalytic activity for removing bisphenol A in water under visible light due to its the dual sensitization effect and double conductive structure of Fe3O4 and carbon.

In this paper, we prepared Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (the mesoporous TiO2 denoted as mTiO2) photocatalyst via a simple synthetic method, and it exhibits strong light response under visible light region because of the dual sensitization effect of Fe3O4 and carbon. In addition, the composite photocatalyst also has double conductivity, which is beneficial to the transfer and separation of charge carriers due to the synergetic effect of Fe3O4, carbon and mTiO2. As a consequence, the double conductive structure Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst displays prominent photocatalytic activity in degradation of BPA under visible light. Furthermore, the photocatalyst not only was recycled easily to reduce the secondary pollution of the environment but also had a superior stability. This work may provide a new inspiration toward constructing dual sensitization and double conductive structure with other semiconductor photocatalyst to effectively improve the photocatalytic activity under visible light for practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: With the fast-growing industry and the rising global economy, bisphenol A (BPA) has been widely served as a raw material for the production of polycarbonate plastics and considered as a representative material among endocrine disruptors to threat the environment and human health [1–3]. Therefore, various efficient methods have been reported for removing BPA from wastewater, such as Fenton-like reaction [4], biological [5], chemical oxidation [6], electrochemical oxidation [7] and photocatalytic method [7–9]. Among all above methods, semiconductor-based photocatalysis [10], which is regarded as a sustainable, eco-friendly and great potential technology, has aroused many interests due to its wide application in wastewater purification and environmental remediation by solar energy.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2), as a typical semiconductor photocatalyst, has been extensively studied as a potential photocatalyst for environment remediation and energy conversion due to its low toxicity, high stability, low cost, and high photocatalytic activity [11–14]. However, common TiO2 has relatively lower photocatalytic efficiency. Recently, it is reported that mesoporous titanium dioxide (mTiO2) has been widely identified as superior photocatalysts for many applications in the fields of adsorption and catalysis due to their tunable structural properties such as surface area, pore volume, size and so forth [15–17]. For example, Zhu et al. [18] prepared mesoporous TiO2 with a high surface area, which exhibits high photocatalytic activity towards degradation of organic pollutants. Zheng et al. [19] synthesized mesoporous TiO2 with remarkably high efficiencies in the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange. But the large band gap of mTiO2 can only absorb ultraviolet light and immensely limits its practical application. Therefore, it is a key issue to endow mTiO2 with visible light photoactivity by widening its light absorption range toward visible light region. In recent years, coupling TiO2 with magnetic Fe3O4 has been reported as an effective way to improve its visible light absorption ability. For example, Yeh et al. [20] synthesized Fe3O4@TiO2 nanoparticles as the binder for fish bacterial pathogen disinfection test under visible light. He et al. [21] prepared a novel core-shell Fe3O4@TiO2 photocatalyst with high photocatalytic activity under visible light. As a result, introduction of Fe3O4 into the mTiO2 system may be a considerable choice because of it not only enhances the light absorption ability from ultraviolet towards visible light but also accelerates the electron transfer, resulting in the enhanced activity of the mTiO2. In addition, the photocatalyst could be effectively separated [22] and recycled to avoid secondary pollution for practical application owing to the Fe3O4 has superior magnetic performance [23–25]. Therefore, the above analysis shows that the mTiO2 combination with magnetic Fe3O4 maybe is a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst.

It is well known that the efficient charge separation plays an important role for improving photocatalytic activity and determining the conversion efficiency of solar energy. It is reported that carbon-based materials can serve as photo-sensitizer, which vastly enhance visible-light absorption. More importantly, the high electron mobility ensures carbon materials with superior electron acceptor and transport channel [26,27]. For instance, Long et al. combined C60 with TiO2 by hydrothermal reaction has high photocatalytic activity under visible light [28]. Wang et al. synthesized hybrid carbon@TiO2 photocatalyst with superior photocatalytic property for practical application [29]. According to the above analysis, coupling with Fe3O4 and carbon materials to fabricate double conductive system maybe effectively improves the photocatalytic performance of mTiO2.

In this work, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst is synthesized successfully via a simple method. The Fe3O4@mTiO2/C presents obviously enhanced absorption from ultraviolet towards visible region, which is attributed to the dual sensitization effect of Fe3O4 and carbon. Meanwhile, the photocatalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity for degradation of BPA under visible light because the double conductive effect of Fe3O4 and carbon could effectively facilitate the separation efficiency of electron-holes pairs. In addition, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst is evaluated by employing various physicochemical characterizations, such as UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, photoluminescent spectrum, fluorescence decay and photoelectrochemical properties and so forth. Furthermore, the as-prepared photocatalysts could be easily separated and reused from solution by applying an external magnetic field. Therefore, this work offers a new insight to develop low-cost and highly efficient visible light photocatalysts for wastewater purification and environmental remediation.
The materials, photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical measurements, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy and characterization were shown in Supporting information.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared via a solvothermal method reported previously [30]. Fe3O4 magnetite particles (3.0 mL, 0.05 g/mL) were dispersed in ethanol (100 mL), and mixed with a concentrated ammonia solution (0.40 mL, 28 wt%) under ultrasound for 15 min. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TIPO) was added dropwise in 5 min, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 24 h at 45 °C under continuous mechanical stirring. The products were separated, collected and then dried at 60 °C in an electric oven.

0.2 g Fe3O4@TiO2 and 0.2 g yeast were dispersed in ethanol (50 mL) under continuous mechanical stirring for 1 h. The products were separated and collected using a magnet, followed by washing with deionized water and ethanol for several times, respectively. Finally, the resultant samples were collected, dried and calcined at 600 °C under nitrogen for 2 h. The preparation process of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst is shown in Scheme1 .

The XRD patterns of the Fe3O4, mTiO2, Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C samples are shown in Fig. 1 . The intense diffraction peaks at 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.2°, 57.1° and 62.7° are corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) plane diffraction of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 88-0135) and the characteristic diffraction peaks at 25.2°, 36.9°, 48.0°, 55.0°, 62.6°, 68.7° and 75.0° are consistent with TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272). In addition, the Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C with the similar diffraction patterns can be well-indexed as Fe3O4 and mTiO2, suggesting that the composite materials are successfully synthesized. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C are slightly weaker than that of the Fe3O4@mTiO2 samples, which affirm that the carbon has almost no influence on the crystal structure of the samples [31]. All the results prove that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite materials are obtained.

The internal microstructural characteristic of as-obtained samples is investigated by the TEM. Fig. 2 a-b obviously proves that the Fe3O4@mTiO2 is core-shell microspheres and layer thickness of mTiO2 is about 35.7 nm. In addition, The TEM image of Fe3O4@TiO2 (Fig. 2d) demonstrates that the Fe3O4@TiO2 has a core-shell structure with light contrast TiO2 shell and dark contrast core of Fe3O4, implying that the Fe3O4 were successfully coated by a TiO2 shell. The high-magnification TEM image of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst further demonstrates the mTiO2 sample is mesoporous structure and the Fe3O4@mTiO2 is successfully loaded on the surface of carbon plate (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the interplanar spacing of the Fe3O4@mTiO2 observed from HRTEM is 0.167 nm and 0.253 nm (Fig. 2e), which agree well with the (211) planes of mTiO2 and (311) planes of Fe3O4 identically. The corresponding FFT pattern (inset of Fig. 2e) displays this sample is single crystal structure and has high crystallinity. Moreover, the EDS elemental maps of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample in Fig. 2f-i exhibit that samples contain C, Fe, O and Ti elements, which are also line in with the EDS analysis in Fig. S1.

The UV–vis DRS spectra are evaluated the light absorption property of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 3 , the DRS spectra of the Fe3O4 presents a widely absorption in full spectrum region. The absorption of mTiO2 shifted from UV light to the visible light region after incorporated with Fe3O4, which means that the Fe3O4 is beneficial to improving the response ability to visible light [21]. In addition, after introduction of carbon, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C further enhances the light absorption ability towards visible light due to the sensitization of carbon. Therefore, we can confirm that the light response range of TiO2 is clearly expanded from ultraviolet towards visible light due to the sensitization effect of Fe3O4, and combination with carbon further prominently improves the light absorption, thus maybe lead to the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C with excellent photocatalytic activities under visible light.

The surface chemical composition and valence states of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample are further determined by XPS measurements. As shown in Fig. S2, the survey XPS spectra display the binding energy peaks are ascribed to Fe 2p, Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s states, which also conforms to the EDS analysis (Fig. S1). In addition, the results of the elemental analysis are shown in Table S1. The weight percentage of C, O, Ti and Fe reached 9.11%, 36.27%, 22.19% and 32.43%, respectively. Furthermore, it obviously shows that the XPS spectrum for Fe 2p (Fig. 4 a) exhibits two peaks at 710.6 and 724.2 eV, which should be assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [32–35]. Moreover, the XPS spectrum of Ti 2p in Fig. 4b are ascribed to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 states at 457.8 and 463.5 eV, respectively. In the spectrum of the C 1s (Fig. 4c), the XPS peaks of the C 1s at 284.1 and 284.8 eV are assigned to the sp2 hybridized carbons and C-C, respectively [36]. As shown in Fig. 4d, it exhibits the peaks at 528.9 and 530.8 are belong to Ti = O and C = O of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C deriving from O 1s state [37–39]. The above results also imply that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst has been successfully synthesized.

It is well known that higher specific surface areas and larger pore volumes of photocatalysts are conduced to enhanced photocatalytic performance owing to the presence of a large amount of surface active sites [40,41]. Fig. 5 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution plots of the different photocatalysts. On the basis of the IUPAC classifications, the isotherm of the mTiO2, Fe3O4@mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C pertains to type IV with an H3 hysteresis loop appearing in the range of 0.4-0.8 (P/P0), proving the presence of mesoporous. This is also determined by the corresponding pore diameter distribution (the inset in Fig. 5a–c), which displays obvious peaks in the mesoporous range (2˜50 nm). The results are conformed to the Fig. 2c. In addition, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (107.5 m2 g−1) is larger than that of mTiO2 (53.9 m2 g−1) and Fe3O4@mTiO2 (77.8 m2 g−1). Therefore, the relative high surface area and the integrated porous structure are also important factors for improved photocatalytic activity.

The magnetization curve of the different samples is investigated by VSM at 300 K. The magnetic hysteresis curves of as-obtained samples are shown in Fig. 6 . The magnetization saturation values (Ms) of the Fe3O4 is 66.5 emu g−1, which confirms the Fe3O4 has superior magnetic performance. In addition, after coating mTiO2 layers (Fe3O4@mTiO2) and combining with carbon (Fe3O4@mTiO2/C), the Ms values are further reduced to 35.9 emu g−1 and 22.6 emu g−1, respectively. Furthermore, it clearly observed that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C has super magnetic property, which makes the photocatalyst recycled easily by an external magnetic field (inset in Fig. 6). These results also indicate that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C is beneficial for practical application.

The photocatalytic activity of different photocatalysts is investigated by degradation of the BPA under visible light. It obviously displays that the BPA has reached the adsorption equilibrium within 30 min in the dark (Fig. S3). In addition, the absorbance variation of Fig. 7 a reduced quickly, which proves that the BPA is decomposed in degradation 120 min by Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst. The result clarifies the BPA molecules are gradually destroyed into small molecules or ions products [42]. The dynamic curves of photodegradation in Fig. 7b show the photocatalytic activity of mTiO2 to degrade BPA is insignificant under visible light. Moreover, the photodegradation ability of mTiO2/C and Fe3O4@mTiO2/C is higher than that of Fe3O4@mTiO2, and the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the kinetic curves of BPA degradation can be approximated as a pseudo-first-order process. Fig. 7c is the plots of -ln(C/C0) versus irradiation time, we notice that the removal rate constant k of BPA over Fe3O4@mTiO2/C are 0.01045 min−1, which reaches about 230.68, 13.08 and 1.73 times as much as that of mTiO2 (0.0000453 min−1), Fe3O4@mTiO2 (0.000799 min−1) and mTiO2/C (0.00603 min−1) in Fig. 7d, respectively. The above results demonstrate that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C has superior photocatalytic activity due to the double conduction effect of Fe3O4 and carbon can effectively facilitate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers.

In consideration of the practical applications of the photocatalysts, it is necessary to evaluate the stability of the as-obtained samples during photodegradation process. Fig. 8 a shows the photocatalytic property of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample for five cycles in BPA degradation tests. The composite photocatalyst exhibits repeatable catalytic activity during five cycles with only a slight decrease, indicating the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C has the excellent stability. In addition, in order to further assess the reusability and stability of the catalysts, the photocatalysts are characterized by XRD before and after five cycles. As shown in Fig. 8b, compared with the original photocatalyst, the XRD pattern of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C has a mild changed after five photocatalytic cycles reaction. The above results exhibit that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst possess prominent photocatalytic recyclability and stability.

It is well known that some active species, such as superoxide radicals (
O2 −), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH) are produced during the photodegradation process. With the purpose of understanding the photocatalytic mechanism of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst, it is essential to confirm which reactive species play an important role in Fe3O4@mTiO2/C sample for photodegradation of BPA under visible light irradiation. In this system, benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA) are used as scavengers for superoxide radicals (
O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and photoexcited holes (h+), respectively [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the photocatalytic activity of BPA obviously decreased when TEOA and BQ are added, indicating
O2 − and h+ are the main reactive species for the decomposition of BPA. In addition, the addition of IPA has a slight effect on the degradation of BPA, suggesting that
OH plays a relatively main role during the photodegradation process. As a result, the order of effect on the photodegradation process for BPA is
O2 − > h+ >
OH. In addition, the active species evolved in the photocatalytic reaction process is carried out with the ESR technique by DMPO. Fig. 9b shows that there are no ESR signals generated in the dark. However, when the light is turned on, the four characteristic peaks of DMPO−
OH are detected in Fe3O4@mTiO2/C, suggesting the
OH generated in the photocatalytic reaction process. Furthermore, from Fig. 9c, the six characteristic peaks of
O2 − existed in the ESR spectra, indicating that the
O2 − radicals also produced during the photocatalytic degradation process. Therefore, the radicals trap experiments and ESR results prove that
O2 −,
OH and h+ are the main active species during the photocatalytic reaction process.

The efficient transmission and separation of photogenerated charge carriers are essential condition for photocatalytic process, it is reported that the intensity of PL emission spectroscopy reflected the recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. [45–47]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the PL intensity of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C is lower than that of mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2, implying the recombination of charge carriers is greatly inhibited of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst. In addition, the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs is further characterized by the lifetime of carriers. It is common knowledge that a longer fluorescent lifetime indicates lower charge carrier recombination rate, resulting in improving photocatalytic activity [48]. The fluorescence decay curves of the different photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 10b, it obviously exhibits that the lifetime of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C (0.74 ns) is longer than that of Fe3O4@mTiO2 (0.56 ns) and mTiO2 (0.41 ns). The above results show that introduction of Fe3O4 and carbon is an effective way to facilitate separation of charge carriers, which contributes to improving the photocatalytic activity.

It is studied that the photocurrent test is used to investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers during the photocatalytic process [49,50], so the higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs over Fe3O4@mTiO2/C is further confirmed by the photocurrent characterization. As shown in Fig. 11 a, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C electrode exhibits the higher photocurrent response than that of mTiO2 and Fe3O4@mTiO2 electrode under visible light irradiation, which attests that the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C can effectively separate electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) of different photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 11b, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C presents the minimum radius of curvature, implying the faster interfacial charge transfer in Fe3O4@mTiO2/C electrode [51] All the results indicate Fe3O4 and carbon in composite materials conduce to improving the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, which results in a tremendous enhancement on photocatalytic activity of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst.

In order to further explore the degradation of BPA mechanism over the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C, the intermediates products in the photocatalytic reaction process are analyzed by Mass spectrometry (MS). As shown in Fig. 12 a-d, compared with the initial solution of BPA with m/z of 227 (Fig. S4), the peak of BPA is gradually reduced with different irradiation time and some new mass spectral peaks appeared, implying the BPA molecules are effectively decomposed into small molecules/ions and produce intermediate products. Furthermore, the primary intermediates products are confirmed at m/z of 133, 243, 241, 135, 184, 257, 109, 170, 275, 143, 173 and 149. As a consequence, based on the above results analysis, a reasonable photodegradation pathway for elimination of BPA over Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 13 . All the above results analysis demonstrated that the original BPA and intermediate products may be gradually degraded to CO2, H2O and other small molecules [8,52,53].

Therefore, on the basis of the above experimental results and theory analyses, the Eg of mTiO2 in Fe3O4@mTiO2/C calculated by a plot of (αhν)2 (Fig. S5). The valence band (VB) potential and conduction band (CB) potential of mTiO2 can be obtained by the Eq. S(1) and Eq. S(2), which are 2.69 V and -0.07 V, respectively. A possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity is shown in Scheme 2 . Obviously, the CB potential of mTiO2 (-0.07 V vs NHE) is more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/
O2 − (Eθ(O2/
O2 −)=-0.046 V vs NHE), which can produce the
O2 − radical [54]. The VB potential of mTiO2 (2.69 V vs NHE) is more positive than the standard redox potential of OH/
OH (Eθ(OH-/
OH)=+1.99 V vs NHE), which satisfies the demand for generation of
OH [54]. Herein, the double conductive of Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst maybe has two ways to separate and transfer electrons. On the one hand, under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of mTiO2 transfer quickly toward the carbon and the electrons react with oxygen in the water to produce
O2 − to remove BPA molecules. On the another hand, a fraction of electrons transferred to Fe3O4, the electrons are captured by Fe(III) ion and transformed into Fe(II) ion, then the Fe(II) ion are involved in the dissolved oxygen in the water to generate Fe(III) ions and
O2 − to eliminate BPA molecules. It is worth noting that Fe(III) ions present in Fe3O4 and it can serve as a photo-excited electron-trapping site to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [55,56]. Meanwhile, a large amount of holes accumulated on the VB of mTiO2 can quickly oxidize the BPA molecules to eco-friendly CO2, H2O and other molecules. Furthermore, a small fraction of hole (h+) can react with OH− to form
OH to degrade BPA, which is consistent with the trapping experiment. The above results reveal that the Fe3O4 and carbon have the double conductive effect to effectively improve the separated efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers, thus enhancing photocatalytic performance of mTiO2.

In summary, we constructed a Fe3O4@mTiO2/C composite photocatalyst with strong visible light response due to the dual sensitization effect of Fe3O4 and carbon. In addition, the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C exhibits the outstanding photocatalytic activity for removing of BPA under visible light irradiation. One possible reason of improved photocatalytic degradation activity is that the dual sensitization effect of Fe3O4 and carbon expands the light absorption ability. Meanwhile, the dual conduction effect of Fe3O4 and carbon also facilitates the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, which is another reason for enhancing photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the magnetic performance of the Fe3O4@mTiO2/C photocatalyst is separated easily and recycled from the solution, which is favourable to the practical application. This work gives a new strategy to expand the optical response towards the visible light region with other semiconductor and to construct double conductive structure system for improving the photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National key research and development program (2016YFB0901600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21407059, 21407064 and 21606114), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20140527, BK20140532 and BK20150536), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. 1501102B), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M641638).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.111902.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
